md: **Conditional probability** asks: 'Given that $B$ has happened, what's the probability that $A$ also happens?' Written $P(A \mid B)$, read as 'probability of $A$ **given** $B$.'
Knowing something restricts the sample space — and that changes the probabilities.
## 1. The Definition
Read it as: 'of the times $B$ happens, what fraction also have $A$?' We restrict to the world where $B$ is true (denominator) and count the cases where $A$ also happens (numerator).
**Example.** Roll a die. Given the result is even, what's the probability it's a 4?
$P(\text{4} \mid \text{even}) = \dfrac{P(\text{4 and even})}{P(\text{even})} = \dfrac{1/6}{3/6} = \dfrac{1}{3}$.
(Of three even outcomes 2, 4, 6, exactly one is a 4.)
## 2. Two-Way Tables
A **two-way table** organizes data into rows and columns by two categorical variables. They make conditional probability calculations very direct.
**Example.** A school survey: 200 students.
$$\begin{array}{c|cc|c} & \text{Likes Math} & \text{Doesn't} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Boys} & 60 & 40 & 100 \\ \text{Girls} & 70 & 30 & 100 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 130 & 70 & 200 \end{array}$$
**Find $P(\text{likes math})$:** $130/200 = 0.65$.
**Find $P(\text{likes math} \mid \text{girl})$:** restrict to the 100 girls. Of those, 70 like math. $70/100 = 0.70$.
**Find $P(\text{girl} \mid \text{likes math})$:** restrict to 130 math-likers. Of those, 70 are girls. $70/130 \approx 0.538$.
Note: $P(\text{girl} \mid \text{math}) \neq P(\text{math} \mid \text{girl})$. **Conditional probability is direction-sensitive.**

blocks.title: Common confusion
blocks.body: $P(A \mid B)$ and $P(B \mid A)$ are usually different! 'P(disease given positive test)' is very different from 'P(positive test given disease)'. The first is what you care about as a patient; the second is what the medical literature publishes about the test's sensitivity. Confusing them is a famous source of bad reasoning ('base rate fallacy').

md: ## 3. Independence Test
Recall: events $A$ and $B$ are **independent** if knowing $B$ doesn't change the probability of $A$. Algebraically:

blocks.tex: A, B \text{ independent} \Leftrightarrow P(A \mid B) = P(A)

md: Equivalently, the multiplicative form: $P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.
**Example from the table above.** Are 'being a girl' and 'liking math' independent?
$P(\text{likes math}) = 0.65$. $P(\text{likes math} \mid \text{girl}) = 0.70$. Different → **NOT independent**.
Intuitively: knowing someone is a girl makes them more likely to like math (in this dataset).
## 4. The Multiplication Rule (Reversed)
Rearranging the conditional definition gives the **general multiplication rule**:

blocks.tex: P(A \text{ and } B) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)

md: This generalizes the 'multiply for independent events' rule: when events are dependent, you multiply by the conditional probability instead of the unconditional one.
### Worked example — medical test
A disease has prevalence $1\%$ in a population. A test is $95\%$ accurate (95% true positive on infected, 95% true negative on healthy).
Someone tests **positive**. What's the probability they actually have the disease?
**Set up using a 10,000-person population:**
**Have disease:** 100 people. Of these, $0.95 \cdot 100 = 95$ test positive (true positives).
**Don't have disease:** 9900 people. Of these, $0.05 \cdot 9900 = 495$ test positive (false positives).
**Total positives:** $95 + 495 = 590$.
**$P(\text{disease} \mid \text{positive}) = 95/590 \approx 0.161$ ≈ 16%.**
Despite the test being '95% accurate', a positive result only means 16% chance of the disease! This is a classic counterintuitive result.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$. Two-way tables make conditional probability easy: restrict to the row/column for the condition, then count. **$P(A|B) \neq P(B|A)$ in general.** Independence iff $P(A|B) = P(A)$.
